W3 Ataiva.history: W3 Ataiva is a website valuation and analysis tool that aggregates publicly available traffic and technical metrics. It appears to be a small solo or startup project with limited public history.
W3 Ataiva.summary_abuse: W3 Ataiva is a web analytics aggregation tool with no notable abuse signal. It scrapes public data about websites and does not provide hosting or communication services that would be targets for fraud.

W3 Total Cache.history: W3 Total Cache was created by Frederick Townes around 2009 as a comprehensive WordPress caching plugin. It became one of the most installed WordPress performance plugins. Liquid Web (a managed hosting company) acquired the plugin's commercial development around 2019, though the free version remains on WordPress.org.
W3 Total Cache.summary_abuse: W3 Total Cache is a WordPress caching plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is backend performance infrastructure for WordPress sites with no consumer-facing surface exploitable by fraudsters.

W3.CSS.history: W3.CSS is a CSS framework developed and maintained by w3schools.com and released around 2016. Despite carrying the W3C name similarity in its branding, it is not an official W3C product but rather a project by the w3schools educational website.
W3.CSS.summary_abuse: W3.CSS is a CSS framework with no abuse signal. It is static stylesheet library code with no hosted service or data-handling surface.

W3Counter.history: W3Counter is a web analytics service founded around 2005. It provides visitor tracking, page-view widgets, and statistics for small websites. It is an older service that has largely been supplanted by Google Analytics but continues to operate.
W3Counter.summary_abuse: W3Counter is a website analytics tracker that embeds a tracking pixel on sites. Like most analytics providers, it collects visitor data across sites where it is embedded, which raises basic privacy considerations but does not constitute notable abuse. No specific fraud or scam associations are documented.

Waaship.history: Waaship is an all-in-one fulfillment and order management SaaS; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Waaship.summary_abuse: Waaship is a B2B fulfillment management platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

Wabi.history: Wabi is a Latin American startup offering WhatsApp-based customer engagement tools for small businesses, helping them sell and communicate via the WhatsApp Business platform.
Wabi.summary_abuse: Wabi is a WhatsApp Business engagement platform primarily used by small merchants. No notable abuse signal in public sources, though WhatsApp-based platforms generally require monitoring for spam and unsolicited messaging.

Wagtail.history: Wagtail was created in 2014 by Torchbox, a UK digital agency, and open-sourced under a BSD license. It quickly gained traction as a developer-friendly CMS built on Django, attracting enterprise users including NASA, Google, and the UK government. Torchbox continues to maintain the core while an active community of contributors has grown around it.
Wagtail.summary_abuse: Wagtail has no notable abuse signal; it is a self-hosted open-source CMS framework with no consumer-facing hosted surface that fraudsters would target directly.

Wair.history: Wair is a personalized fit recommendation widget for fashion e-commerce; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Wair.summary_abuse: Wair is a personalized sizing recommendation widget with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B embedded tool for fashion retail.

waitForImages.history: waitForImages is a small JavaScript library created by Alexander Dickson around 2012 to simplify jQuery-based image preload event handling. The project appears to have been dormant for several years with no notable ownership changes.
waitForImages.summary_abuse: waitForImages is a lightweight JavaScript image preloading library with no notable abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing surface and is used purely as a utility in web development.

Waitlist.history: Waitlist (getwaitlist.com) is a SaaS tool launched around 2020 that helps startups and businesses create referral-based waitlists for product launches.
Waitlist.summary_abuse: Waitlist is a lead-capture SaaS used by legitimate product launches. Low abuse risk; the main theoretical concern is using free-tier waitlist pages to harvest emails, but this is a minor and uncommon pattern.

Wakav Performance Monitoring.history: Wakav Performance Monitoring is an Iranian real user monitoring and web performance platform. Limited public history available.
Wakav Performance Monitoring.summary_abuse: Wakav Performance Monitoring is a web performance monitoring tool with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

Wake.history: Wake Commerce was launched in April 2023 as a new brand by Locaweb Company, Brazil's major web hosting and cloud computing firm. Wake unifies six acquisitions made by Locaweb, including Marketing All In, Squid, Síntese, Íderis, Samurai Experts, and the Tray e-commerce platform. The brand consolidates these companies into an integrated ecosystem (Wake Commerce, Wake Experience, and Squid by Wake) targeting medium-to-large e-commerce businesses in Brazil.
Wake.summary_abuse: Wake is a B2B ecommerce platform backed by Locaweb, serving medium-to-large Brazilian retailers. The platform targets enterprise-grade merchants and is not freely available for anonymous or low-friction signups. No documented phishing, fraud, or scam hosting incidents are associated with Wake.

Wake Commerce.history: Wake Commerce is the headless commerce arm of Wake (wake.tech), a Brazilian e-commerce technology company. It targets enterprise retailers with a composable/headless architecture.
Wake Commerce.summary_abuse: Wake Commerce is a headless e-commerce platform for enterprise retailers in Brazil. No notable abuse signal in public sources.

WalkMe.history: WalkMe was founded in 2011 in Tel Aviv by Dan Adika and Rafael Sweary to provide digital adoption and in-app guidance. It went public on the Nasdaq in 2021. In 2023, SAP acquired WalkMe for approximately $1.5 billion, integrating it into SAP's enterprise software portfolio.
WalkMe.summary_abuse: WalkMe has no notable abuse signal; it is an enterprise digital adoption platform embedded by IT teams into internal or customer-facing applications. It collects user interaction analytics but is deployed and controlled by enterprise customers under contract.

Walla.history: Walla Software was founded in 2020 by Laura Munkholm and Matthew Ausonio in San Diego, California. The company provides cloud-based fitness studio management software targeting boutique fitness studios (yoga, pilates, barre, cycling, and multi-location franchises). Walla raised $18M in total funding, including a $13M Series A round in July 2022 backed by TenOneTen Ventures.
Walla.summary_abuse: Walla is a fitness studio management platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform serves boutique fitness studios for scheduling, billing, and analytics, with no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud. No documented abuse cases are associated with the service.

Wallkit.history: Wallkit is a subscription management and paywall platform for publishers and media organizations; no notable ownership changes are publicly documented.
Wallkit.summary_abuse: Wallkit is a subscription paywall management platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves media organizations and has no consumer-facing content hosting features.

Wally.history: Wally is a review aggregation tool importing and displaying reviews from Google, Trustpilot, and similar platforms; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Wally.summary_abuse: Wally is a review display tool with no notable abuse signal. Review aggregation tools can theoretically be used to display fake reviews, but Wally imports from established review platforms.

Wangsu.history: Wangsu Science and Technology (also known as ChinaCache) was founded in 1998 and became one of China's major CDN and internet infrastructure providers. It operates as a publicly listed company in China. In 2016 it rebranded and expanded its global CDN offerings. It is a key CDN provider for Chinese enterprises.
Wangsu.summary_abuse: Wangsu is a Chinese CDN provider. CDN infrastructure can be used to distribute malicious content or serve as infrastructure for phishing campaigns, and Chinese CDNs have been noted in some security research as hosting points for malicious traffic, though legitimate use vastly dominates. The geopolitical context means some western security tools flag Chinese CDN traffic with elevated scrutiny.

wap.store.history: wap.store was founded in 2005 by Luciano Sanches and Nélio Camargo in Birigui, São Paulo, Brazil. The company initially focused on creating e-commerce websites before launching the wap.store platform in 2013. The platform has grown to process over 7.2 million orders annually across its merchant base. The company subsequently rebranded to uappi, though it continues to operate under the wap.store name in the market.
wap.store.summary_abuse: wap.store is a Brazilian ecommerce platform with a modest abuse risk consistent with other regional ecommerce platforms. No specific documented incidents of phishing, fraud, or scam hosting are attributed to wap.store. Brazil's elevated ecommerce fraud environment is a general risk factor, but no vendor-specific patterns have been reported.

WAR.PE.history: WAR.PE is a privacy and security platform for web users. Limited public history is available; it appears to be a small startup.
WAR.PE.summary_abuse: WAR.PE is a privacy-focused web security tool with no documented abuse signal. Its stated purpose is user protection rather than providing infrastructure that could be exploited for fraud.

Warm Welcome.history: Warm Welcome is a video engagement SaaS launched around 2020, offering interactive video bubbles and video email for customer engagement and onboarding.
Warm Welcome.summary_abuse: Warm Welcome is a video engagement widget tool with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

Warmly.history: Warmly is an AI-powered sales intelligence and prospect automation platform founded around 2021, focused on identifying and engaging anonymous website visitors for B2B sales teams.
Warmly.summary_abuse: Warmly is a B2B sales intelligence tool that identifies website visitors using IP lookups and intent data. Privacy-conscious users may object to visitor deanonymization, but it is used by legitimate sales teams rather than for fraud.

Warp.history: Warp is a Haskell web server library created around 2010 by the Yesod web framework community, providing high-performance HTTP serving capabilities for Haskell web applications.
Warp.summary_abuse: Warp is a Haskell web server library with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Warp Store.history: Warp Store is a website creation platform for game-related merchandise stores; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Warp Store.summary_abuse: Warp Store is a niche storefront builder for gaming merchandise. E-commerce platforms can be used for fake stores, but Warp Store has no specific documented abuse incidents.

Warply.history: Warply is a Greek customer loyalty and engagement platform that provides businesses with tools for rewards, coupons, and customer data management. It has operated primarily in the banking and retail sectors.
Warply.summary_abuse: Warply is a customer loyalty platform used by banks and retailers in Greece. No notable abuse signal in public sources.

Wask.history: Wask is a Turkish digital marketing and ad management SaaS platform founded around 2018, offering tools for analyzing and optimizing web and social media ad performance.
Wask.summary_abuse: Wask is a digital advertising analytics and management platform. No notable abuse signal in public sources.

Waste Connections.history: Waste Connections, Inc. is a publicly traded waste management company incorporated in 1997, operating in North America with hundreds of collection operations. Its website platform is used primarily for customer service and account management for municipal waste services.
Waste Connections.summary_abuse: Waste Connections is the corporate website of a large public waste management company. No abuse signal relevant to web technology.

WatchThem.history: WatchThemLive was founded in 2020 and is headquartered in Toronto, Canada. The company provides session recording, heatmap tracking, and behavioral analytics for websites, serving customers in over 180 countries.
WatchThem.summary_abuse: WatchThemLive is a session recording and behavioral analytics platform. The tool records real visitor sessions, including keystrokes and mouse movements, which raises privacy considerations but is standard in the analytics category. No specific abuse incidents are documented, though improper installation on sites without user consent would violate privacy regulations.

Wati.history: Wati was founded in 2020 in Hong Kong as a WhatsApp Business API platform enabling businesses to manage customer conversations at scale. It raised venture funding and expanded rapidly across Asian and global markets.
Wati.summary_abuse: Wati is a WhatsApp Business API platform used by businesses to send and receive messages at scale. While designed for legitimate customer support and marketing, mass messaging platforms built on WhatsApp can be misused for spam or unsolicited bulk messages. Wati has usage policies and operates under WhatsApp's own API terms which include anti-spam requirements.

Wave Commerce.history: Wave Commerce is a Hong Kong-based Shopify development and digital marketing agency serving brands in Asia.
Wave Commerce.summary_abuse: Wave Commerce is a Shopify development and digital marketing agency with no notable abuse signal.

Waveform.history: Waveform is a media player widget supporting audio, video, YouTube, and other formats for website embedding; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Waveform.summary_abuse: Waveform is a media player widget with no abuse signal. It is an embedded player with no consumer-facing content hosting capabilities.

Waveme.history: Waveme is a WordPress theme for music platforms created by Flatfull, a small theme developer selling through ThemeForest. It is designed for music streaming, DJ, and audio publisher websites.
Waveme.summary_abuse: Waveme has no notable abuse signal. It is a WordPress theme for music websites sold as a one-time purchase through ThemeForest. It is self-hosted software with no platform surface that would attract fraudulent use.

WayForPay.history: WayForPay is a Ukrainian payment processing company founded around 2013, providing payment gateway services primarily for Ukrainian and Eastern European e-commerce businesses.
WayForPay.summary_abuse: WayForPay is a Ukrainian payment processor. Payment gateways can be misused for fraudulent transactions, but WayForPay operates under standard financial regulation and PCI-DSS compliance requirements. No specific large-scale fraud incidents attributed to the platform are documented publicly.

Wazala.history: Wazala was an embeddable shopping cart SaaS product launched around 2010, allowing website owners to add an online store to any website. It appears to have declined in activity over time.
Wazala.summary_abuse: Wazala is an older embeddable shopping cart product with no notable abuse signal. It allows store creation on websites but serves legitimate merchants.

Wazimo.history: Wazimo was founded in Israel as a digital media and real-time bidding (RTB) advertising technology company focused on audience engagement and monetization. In November 2021, Wazimo was acquired by Minute Media, a global sports and entertainment content company, for a reported value in the range of $60-80 million.
Wazimo.summary_abuse: Wazimo is an ad-tech platform focused on RTB and audience monetization. As an advertising network, it places third-party tracking pixels and ad scripts on publisher sites, which is the core mechanism of the ad-tech ecosystem rather than abuse per se. No documented use in phishing or scam infrastructure. Its privacy risk is primarily from extensive audience data collection inherent to the RTB model.

wBuy.history: wBuy (formerly Brasil Na Web / BNW) is a Brazilian SaaS ecommerce platform founded around 2005. The company is headquartered in Brazil with offices in Maringá, Rio de Janeiro, Belo Horizonte, Florianópolis, and Porto Alegre. In recent years the platform rebranded from BNW/Brasil Na Web to wBuy, while retaining all existing customer data and functionality. The platform is powered by AWS infrastructure and is certified as a Stone (payment) partner.
wBuy.summary_abuse: wBuy is a Brazilian ecommerce SaaS platform with a modest abuse risk. As an ecommerce platform serving Brazilian small businesses, it could theoretically be used to set up fraudulent storefronts, though no specific documented incidents are publicly attributed to wBuy. Brazil's ecommerce market has elevated consumer fraud rates, but no evidence suggests wBuy is a preferred platform for such activity.

Weatherstack.history: Weatherstack is a real-time weather data API launched around 2019 by Apilayer, a company building a portfolio of developer API products. It provides global weather data via JSON/XML for developers embedding weather functionality into applications.
Weatherstack.summary_abuse: Weatherstack is a weather data API with no notable abuse signal. It provides read-only weather data for application developers and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract fraud.

Weaver Xtreme.history: Weaver Xtreme is a long-standing options-based WordPress theme created by a solo developer, with roots going back to around 2011. It has maintained a loyal user base among those who prefer highly customizable themes without page builders.
Weaver Xtreme.summary_abuse: Weaver Xtreme is a WordPress theme with no notable abuse signal.

Weaverse.history: Weaverse is a Shopify Hydrogen theme customizer and headless CMS launched around 2022, targeting Shopify developers building modern headless storefronts.
Weaverse.summary_abuse: Weaverse is a Shopify theme customization tool with no notable abuse signal.

Web CEO.history: Web CEO was founded in 1999 as one of the early SEO software tools, initially as a desktop application before evolving into a cloud-based SEO and digital marketing platform used by agencies and in-house marketers.
Web CEO.summary_abuse: Web CEO is an SEO and digital marketing analytics platform with no notable abuse signal.

Web Font Loader.history: Web Font Loader was originally created by Google and Typekit (now Adobe Fonts) as a joint project around 2011, providing JavaScript-based font loading control for web fonts. The repository is hosted under Typekit/Adobe's GitHub and has been in maintenance-only/dormant state for several years as native CSS font-loading APIs matured.
Web Font Loader.summary_abuse: Web Font Loader is a JavaScript font-loading library with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Web Shop Manager.history: Web Shop Manager is an automotive ecommerce platform based in San Diego, California, claiming over 25 years of experience in the industry. The company specializes in aftermarket automotive parts ecommerce, offering native Year-Make-Model (YMM) search, ACES/PIES fitment data, and headless architecture. The platform has served over 1,000 automotive businesses worldwide.
Web Shop Manager.summary_abuse: Web Shop Manager is a specialized automotive ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform serves legitimate auto parts retailers and requires merchant contracts. No documented phishing, fraud, or scam hosting incidents are associated with the service.

Web Stories.history: Web Stories is a visual storytelling format introduced by Google in 2018 as part of AMP for immersive tappable stories on the open web.
Web Stories.summary_abuse: Web Stories is an open web storytelling standard with no notable abuse signal.

Web Stories for WordPress.history: Web Stories for WordPress is a plugin developed by Google that brings the Web Stories visual storytelling format to WordPress, launched in 2020 as an open-source plugin.
Web Stories for WordPress.summary_abuse: Web Stories for WordPress is an open-source storytelling plugin developed by Google with no notable abuse signal.

Web Wiz Forums.history: Web Wiz Forums is a classic ASP-based web forum software created in the UK around 2001, one of the early free forum packages that enabled community discussion boards on self-hosted websites.
Web Wiz Forums.summary_abuse: Web Wiz Forums is a self-hosted forum software. Forum software historically can be a target for spam bots posting links, but the platform itself has no consumer-facing surface beyond what administrators deploy on their own sites.

Web Worker Offloading.history: Web Worker Offloading is a WordPress plugin from the WordPress Performance Team, developed with Google support, released around 2024 to improve page performance by offloading JavaScript execution to web workers.
Web Worker Offloading.summary_abuse: Web Worker Offloading is a performance plugin from the WordPress core team with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

web-vitals.history: web-vitals was created by the Google Chrome team and first published in 2020 as an open-source JavaScript library to help developers measure Core Web Vitals metrics (LCP, FID, CLS) on real users. It was developed to support Google's Web Vitals initiative and the related search ranking signals introduced in the 2021 Page Experience update.
web-vitals.summary_abuse: web-vitals is a Google Chrome team library for measuring browser performance metrics and has no notable abuse signal. It collects performance data about the page it runs on and sends nothing to external servers by default; any reporting is controlled by the implementing developer.

Web2py.history: Web2py is a Python web framework created by Massimo Di Pierro, first released in 2007. It was designed with ease of use and no-installation simplicity in mind, including a built-in IDE. It became popular in academic and research settings but has seen declining adoption as Django and Flask became dominant.
Web2py.summary_abuse: Web2py is a Python web framework with no notable abuse signal beyond the generic exposure of any self-hosted web application framework.

Web3Forms.history: Web3Forms was founded in 2020 by Surjith S M in Palakkad, Kerala, India, as a serverless contact form backend for static and JAMstack websites. Despite its name, it has no connection to blockchain technology — the name references its use of an API key system. The solo founder built it to address the lack of simple, free form backends for static-site developers.
Web3Forms.summary_abuse: Web3Forms is a form-to-email backend service with no notable abuse signal targeting end consumers. The primary abuse risk is form spam, which is a standard issue for all form backends; the service provides reCAPTCHA and hCaptcha integration to mitigate this. No documented cases of the service being used to host phishing pages or facilitate scams.

Web4Realty.history: Web4Realty was founded in 2009 by Raza and Kosta Panagoulias in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada. The company provides a cloud-based marketing and sales platform for real estate brokers and agents, including CRM, website builder, email marketing, workflow automation, and lead management. By 2020 the company had grown to approximately $12M in revenue with a 15-person team, serving thousands of agents across Canada and the United States.
Web4Realty.summary_abuse: Web4Realty is a real estate marketing SaaS platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform is used by licensed real estate professionals and requires agent or broker registration. No documented phishing, fraud, or scam incidents are associated with the service.

Webareal.history: Webareal is a Czech e-commerce platform founded around 2010 offering online store creation and management tools for small businesses in the Czech and Slovak markets.
Webareal.summary_abuse: Webareal is a Czech e-commerce SaaS with no notable abuse signal.

WebAssembly.history: WebAssembly (Wasm) was developed collaboratively by Mozilla, Google, Apple, and Microsoft, with the MVP specification released in 2017 and ratified as a W3C standard in 2019. It enables near-native performance for web applications by providing a binary instruction format that runs in browsers and server-side runtimes.
WebAssembly.summary_abuse: WebAssembly is a foundational binary instruction format standard. It has been used in some instances for cryptomining scripts in browsers due to its performance characteristics, though this reflects misuse by website operators rather than a flaw in the standard itself.

Webasyst Shop-Script.history: Webasyst Shop-Script is a PHP e-commerce framework and shopping cart system developed by Russian company Webasyst, targeting small-to-medium online stores in Russian-speaking markets.
Webasyst Shop-Script.summary_abuse: Webasyst Shop-Script is a self-hosted e-commerce framework. No notable abuse signal in public sources, though its Russian origin may concern some compliance-focused enterprises.

WebAwesome.history: Web Awesome is an open-source web components library created by Font Awesome, released around 2023, building on the earlier Shoelace component library to provide standards-based, framework-agnostic UI components.
WebAwesome.summary_abuse: Web Awesome is an open-source UI component library with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

WebBoss.history: WebBoss was founded in 2011 and is based in London, UK. It targets professional designers and developers wanting a flexible CMS with built-in hosting and e-commerce modules. No notable ownership changes or major events are documented.
WebBoss.summary_abuse: WebBoss is a niche UK website builder targeting professional developers with no notable abuse signal. The platform requires a paid subscription with no anonymous free tier, which significantly limits its attractiveness for phishing or scam page hosting.

Webbotify.history: Webbotify is a post-2022 AI chatbot builder that trains chatbots on website content; it represents a new wave of ChatGPT-based website chatbot tools.
Webbotify.summary_abuse: Webbotify is an AI chatbot builder wrapping LLM APIs. Like similar tools, there is theoretical risk of misuse for social engineering, but no specific documented incidents for Webbotify.

Webcake.history: Webcake is a landing page builder and ecommerce platform primarily targeting the Vietnamese market. An earlier Y Combinator-backed WebCake project by Nathan Speller in 2013 appears to be separate and inactive; the current webcake.io is the active product.
Webcake.summary_abuse: Webcake is a landing page and e-commerce builder with a free tier. The free tier with hosted pages is a moderate risk vector for scam landing pages, though no specific documented incidents are known.

Webcool CMS.history: Webcool CMS is a Vietnamese content management and workflow automation platform targeting businesses in Vietnam and Southeast Asia.
Webcool CMS.summary_abuse: Webcool CMS is a Vietnamese CMS platform with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

WEBDEV.history: WEBDEV is a RAD (Rapid Application Development) tool developed by PC SOFT in France, first released in the mid-1990s. It is a long-standing product in the French-speaking business application development market, used primarily for building intranet and database-driven web applications.
WEBDEV.summary_abuse: WEBDEV is a proprietary RAD IDE for enterprise application development with no abuse signal. It is developer tooling targeted at French-speaking enterprise developers and has no consumer-facing surface.

webEdition.history: webEdition is a German CMS developed and sold by webEdition Software GmbH. It has been in use primarily in the German-speaking market for managing websites and content, particularly among mid-size German businesses. The product appears to have low active development in recent years.
webEdition.summary_abuse: webEdition is a niche German CMS with no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted CMS used by legitimate organizations and does not provide consumer-facing hosting features that would attract phishing or scam activity.

WebEngage.history: WebEngage was founded in 2011 in Mumbai, India, as a customer data platform and marketing automation suite targeting mid-market and enterprise businesses, particularly in emerging markets. It raised significant VC funding and became one of India's prominent marketing technology companies.
WebEngage.summary_abuse: WebEngage is an enterprise marketing automation and customer data platform. It collects user behavioral data for personalized marketing, which carries standard data-privacy considerations, but no notable abuse incidents involving the platform itself are documented.

Webeo.history: Webeo is a UK-based B2B website personalization platform founded around 2015 that uses company data to dynamically customize website content for different business visitors.
Webeo.summary_abuse: Webeo is a B2B website personalization tool that uses IP-to-company data. Privacy-aware users may note that this involves passive tracking of visitors, but the tool targets legitimate B2B marketing use cases and no fraud incidents are documented.

Weberlo.history: Weberlo is a marketing ROI and ROAS tracking analytics platform; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Weberlo.summary_abuse: Weberlo is a marketing analytics platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

Webestools.history: Webestools is a French website offering free online web tools, counters, and widgets primarily used by small websites and personal pages. It has operated since the mid-2000s.
Webestools.summary_abuse: Webestools provides free counters and web widgets for small sites. Its free tools with minimal friction could be embedded in low-quality pages, but no notable abuse incidents are documented.

Webeyez.history: Webeyez is an Israeli e-commerce monitoring and analytics platform founded around 2018, providing technical monitoring and UX analytics to help e-commerce teams identify and fix issues impacting revenue.
Webeyez.summary_abuse: Webeyez is an e-commerce analytics and monitoring platform with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

WebFactory Maintenance.history: WebFactory Maintenance is a WordPress plugin by WebFactory Ltd, a Croatian WordPress specialist studio founded in 2009 that has produced 150+ plugins with over 1 million users. The plugin enables maintenance mode for WordPress sites and is distributed through the official WordPress plugin repository.
WebFactory Maintenance.summary_abuse: WebFactory Maintenance is a WordPress plugin enabling maintenance mode pages with no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted plugin with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

WebFactory Under Construction.history: WebFactory Under Construction is a WordPress plugin by WebFactory Ltd, a Croatian WordPress specialist studio founded in 2009. It enables coming-soon and under-construction pages for WordPress sites and has been installed over 1 million times from the WordPress plugin repository.
WebFactory Under Construction.summary_abuse: WebFactory Under Construction is a WordPress plugin that renders coming-soon pages; it has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted plugin with no hosted surface that fraudsters would target.

Webflow.history: Webflow was founded in 2013 in San Francisco by Vlad Magdalin, Sergie Magdalin, and Bryant Chou. It grew rapidly as a no-code visual web builder and raised substantial VC funding, reaching a $4 billion valuation in 2022. It is now one of the leading no-code website platforms used by designers and marketers worldwide.
Webflow.summary_abuse: Webflow's free tier and ease of use have made it an occasional target for phishing page creation and fake storefronts, a pattern common to all major website builders. Webflow has an abuse reporting channel and AUP, though as a popular platform some incidents do occur before removal.

Webflow Ecommerce.history: Webflow Ecommerce was launched in 2019 as the commerce layer of the Webflow no-code platform, allowing users to build and manage online stores visually without code.
Webflow Ecommerce.summary_abuse: Webflow Ecommerce inherits the same abuse profile as the broader Webflow platform. Fake storefronts are an occasional concern given the platform's ease of use, but Webflow maintains an AUP and abuse reporting process.

Webgains.history: Webgains is a UK-based affiliate marketing network founded in 2005, part of the ad pepper media group. It operates across Europe and globally, connecting advertisers with publisher affiliates.
Webgains.summary_abuse: Webgains is an affiliate marketing network. Affiliate networks can be exploited by fraudulent publishers using cookie stuffing, fake traffic, or spam links, though Webgains has fraud detection mechanisms. Affiliate tracking pixels also serve as a form of user tracking across partner sites.

WebGUI.history: WebGUI is an open-source Perl-based content management system created around 2001 by Plain Black Corporation. It was used in enterprise and educational settings but has become largely dormant as CMS alternatives gained dominance.
WebGUI.summary_abuse: WebGUI is a legacy open-source CMS with no notable abuse signal; it is rarely encountered in modern deployments.

Webhealer.history: Webhealer is a UK-based CMS platform specifically designed for therapists and healthcare practitioners to create and manage their professional websites, operating since around 2000.
Webhealer.summary_abuse: Webhealer is a niche CMS for therapists and healthcare practitioners with no notable abuse signal.

WebHostUK.history: WebHostUK is a UK-based shared web hosting provider offering shared hosting, VPS, and domain services targeting small businesses and individuals at competitive price points.
WebHostUK.summary_abuse: WebHostUK is a small UK shared hosting provider. Like most budget shared hosting providers, its infrastructure can be used to host malicious content or low-cost phishing pages; the company's AUP and abuse responsiveness are not prominently documented.

Webica.history: Webica is a web-based advertising campaign management platform; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Webica.summary_abuse: Webica is an advertising campaign management platform with no notable abuse signal. Ad platforms can serve as vectors for fraudulent ad campaigns, but no specific incidents are documented for Webica.

Webim.history: Webim is a Russian live chat and customer support platform founded around 2011, providing customer communication tools for websites primarily in Russian-speaking markets.
Webim.summary_abuse: Webim is a Russian live chat platform. Its Russian origin may raise compliance concerns in some jurisdictions, but no notable direct abuse signal is documented for the platform.

Webinaris.history: Webinaris is a German automated webinar platform headquartered in Graefelfing, Germany. The company provides tools for automating sales webinars and customer acquisition, with features like automated email sequences, landing pages, and CRM integrations. Founded around 2007, it primarily serves the German-speaking market.
Webinaris.summary_abuse: Webinaris is an automated webinar platform primarily used for digital sales presentations. Automated webinar platforms can be misused for fake investment pitches, course scams, or lead generation fraud, though no specific incidents are documented for Webinaris. The platform requires account setup and payment, which limits the most opportunistic fraud use.

Webinato.history: Webinato is a webinar automation and streaming platform founded around 2010, providing automated webinar scheduling and management capabilities.
Webinato.summary_abuse: Webinato is a webinar automation platform with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

Webito.history: Webito is an AI-powered website builder for creating scalable sites; it is a post-2022 product in a crowded market of AI site builders.
Webito.summary_abuse: Webito is an AI website builder. Website builders with free hosting tiers can be exploited to create phishing pages, though no specific abuse incidents are documented for Webito.

Webix.history: Webix is a JavaScript UI component library developed by XB Software since around 2012, providing over 100 UI widgets for building enterprise web applications. It uses a dual-license model with a free community edition and a commercial paid version.
Webix.summary_abuse: Webix is a UI component library with no consumer-facing hosted surface and no notable abuse signal.

Weblate.history: Weblate was created by Michal Cihar in 2012 as an open-source continuous localization platform built on Django. It grew into one of the most widely used open-source translation management systems, with the maintainer founding Weblate s.r.o. in the Czech Republic to provide hosted services and commercial support.
Weblate.summary_abuse: Weblate is an open-source localization platform with no notable abuse signal. Its self-hosted option and open-source nature mean it is used legitimately by thousands of projects.

Weblication.history: Weblication is a German enterprise CMS developed since around 2005, focused on serving medium-to-large businesses in the DACH region with web content management.
Weblication.summary_abuse: Weblication is a German enterprise CMS with no notable abuse signal.

Weblium.history: Weblium is a Ukrainian website builder and online store platform founded around 2017, providing AI-assisted website creation for small businesses.
Weblium.summary_abuse: Weblium is a Ukrainian website builder with a free tier. Like other free builders, it carries theoretical risk of fake page creation but no notable documented incidents.

WeBlocks.history: WeBlocks is an educational platform for building JavaScript and Webflow projects; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
WeBlocks.summary_abuse: WeBlocks is a development education platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

Weblogic Server.history: WebLogic Server was originally developed by BEA Systems starting in 1995, becoming one of the leading Java EE application servers. BEA was acquired by Oracle in 2008 for approximately $8.5 billion. Oracle has continued developing it as part of Oracle Fusion Middleware, and it remains a major enterprise application server in large-scale Java deployments.
Weblogic Server.summary_abuse: Oracle WebLogic Server is an enterprise application server with no consumer-facing surface. It has historically been targeted by security vulnerabilities (CVEs) due to its widespread enterprise deployment, but this reflects targeted exploitation rather than abuse in the sense of fraud or scams.

WebMetric.history: WebMetric (webmetric.ir) is an Iranian digital marketing analytics platform offering heatmaps, sales funnels, campaign tracking, and marketing automation based on big data and machine learning. It is primarily used in the Iranian market.
WebMetric.summary_abuse: WebMetric is an analytics and marketing tool for Iranian websites with no notable documented abuse signal in international security research.

Webmin.history: Webmin was created by Jamie Cameron and first released in 1997 as a free web-based Linux server administration interface. It became one of the most widely used server management tools for Linux, though it faced a major security incident in 2018 when a backdoor was discovered in a compromised distribution mirror.
Webmin.summary_abuse: Webmin is a server administration interface that has been targeted by attackers when exposed to the internet. In 2018 a backdoor was found in a malicious version distributed via SourceForge. Server admin panels exposed publicly are often targeted by brute-force attacks. However, this reflects how the tool is targeted rather than the tool being used to commit abuse.

WebNode.history: Webnode was founded in 2008 in the Czech Republic as a drag-and-drop website builder. It grew significantly in emerging markets and Europe, offering multilingual website creation tools with a free tier and affordable paid plans.
WebNode.summary_abuse: Webnode is a website builder with a free tier that allows creating websites quickly. Like other free website builders, it has been occasionally used for fake storefronts or phishing pages due to its low friction signup and custom subdomain hosting. The platform's abuse enforcement is not prominently documented.

WeboLead.history: WeboLead is a French B2B lead intelligence platform that deanonymizes website visitors using IP-to-company data for sales prospecting.
WeboLead.summary_abuse: WeboLead is a visitor deanonymization tool for B2B sales. It involves passive visitor tracking which may concern privacy-focused users, but is used for legitimate B2B prospecting rather than fraud.

Webolytics.history: Webolytics is an open API ROAS tracking platform for affiliate programs; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Webolytics.summary_abuse: Webolytics is an advertising spend tracking platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

Webotit.history: Webotit is a French generative AI platform providing chatbot, callbot, and mailbot solutions for business customer service automation.
Webotit.summary_abuse: Webotit is a B2B AI chatbot and automation platform with no notable abuse signal.

Webpack.history: Webpack was created by Tobias Koppers and first released in 2012 as a JavaScript module bundler. It quickly became the de facto bundler for large-scale JavaScript applications and is backed by the OpenJS Foundation. While newer bundlers like Vite and esbuild have gained traction, webpack remains one of the most widely used bundlers in enterprise environments.
Webpack.summary_abuse: Webpack is a foundational JavaScript build tool with no abuse signal. It runs entirely in local development and CI environments, has no consumer-facing surface, and is not a vector for phishing or malware hosting.

Webpushr.history: Webpushr was founded around 2015 as a web push notification SaaS. It offers a free tier supporting up to 10,000 subscribers and has grown as an independent company with no notable acquisitions or rebrands.
Webpushr.summary_abuse: Webpushr is a web push notification platform that could be misused to send spam or phishing notifications to subscribers. Push notification platforms carry moderate abuse risk because malicious actors can use free tiers to send unsolicited or deceptive browser notifications. Webpushr requires domain verification, which mitigates some abuse, but the free tier and lack of documented anti-abuse enforcement keep risk at a moderate level.

WebQnA.history: WebQnA is an AI-powered chatbot platform founded around 2023, based in Vasai, India. It offers smart Q&A chatbots integrating GPT models for customer support across web, Microsoft Teams, Slack, and Facebook Messenger.
WebQnA.summary_abuse: WebQnA is a B2B AI chatbot platform with no notable abuse signal. It requires direct sales contact, limiting anonymous abuse.

Webready.history: Webready was founded around 2018 as Webready Inc., a Delaware corporation. It was created to provide vacation rental property owners with a purpose-built website builder that enables direct bookings without third-party platform fees. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions have occurred.
Webready.summary_abuse: Webready is a vacation rental website builder with moderate abuse potential due to its free tier with custom domain support, which could be exploited to create fraudulent rental listing sites. The platform is relatively niche and small-scale with no documented widespread abuse campaigns. The vendor's Terms of Service include an Acceptable Use Policy and grounds for account termination in cases of abuse. No notable public security incidents have been reported.

Webreed.history: Founded in 2017 in France by Julie and Vincent as a web-based breeding management tool for cat and dog breeders. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Webreed.summary_abuse: Webreed is a niche SaaS for professional pet breeders with no meaningful abuse signal. It does not host arbitrary user-generated content, offer anonymous signup, or expose features commonly targeted by fraudsters. As a French company, it is subject to GDPR and operates within European regulatory frameworks.

WEBrick.history: WEBrick is a lightweight HTTP server library bundled with Ruby's standard library since around 2000. It was commonly used for local development of Ruby web applications but has largely been superseded by Puma and other production-grade servers. It is still included in Ruby's standard library.
WEBrick.summary_abuse: WEBrick is a Ruby standard library HTTP server used for local development with no abuse signal. It is not deployed in production internet-facing environments and has no consumer surface exploitable for fraud.

Webriti Busiprof.history: Published in 2013 as a WordPress theme by Webriti; it has remained on the market as a free lite version plus a paid pro offering, with no notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Webriti Busiprof.summary_abuse: Busiprof has no notable standalone abuse signal; it is a WordPress theme, so scammers could use it as the front end for fake sites, but it provides no hosting, messaging, or redirect infrastructure itself and there is no public abuse pattern specific to the theme.

WebRTC.history: WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) was open-sourced by Google in 2011 after acquiring Global IP Solutions (GIPS) in 2010. It was standardized by the W3C and IETF, becoming the foundation of browser-native audio/video communication. It is now a core component of major video conferencing platforms.
WebRTC.summary_abuse: WebRTC is a foundational real-time communication standard used by video conferencing, collaboration, and streaming platforms worldwide. No notable abuse signal inherent to the protocol.

Websale.history: Founded in 1999 as a cloud-native ecommerce platform in Nuremberg, Germany. Operates as WEBSALE AG, an owner-managed company with no external investors, serving over 500 enterprise midmarket shops across Europe. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions.
Websale.summary_abuse: WEBSALE is a German enterprise ecommerce SaaS platform with low abuse risk. The service requires identity verification, has a minimum monthly fee of 500 EUR, and hosts all data in ISO 27001-certified German data centers. No notable abuse incidents or scam patterns documented.

Webscribble.history: Web Scribble was co-founded in 2002 by Alexey Gutin and Alexander Gutin. The company is headquartered in Boca Raton, Florida, and serves over 1,000 association and higher-education customers with career center and job board software. It remains privately held with no notable acquisition or rebranding events.
Webscribble.summary_abuse: Web Scribble is a career center and job board platform for associations and higher education with negligible abuse surface. It is a hosted SaaS solution with no consumer-facing free tier, no custom domain hosting, no anonymous signup, and no file hosting capabilities. The platform's enterprise-only sales model and focus on mission-driven organizations make it unattractive for fraudulent use. No notable abuse incidents are documented.

Website Creator.history: Founded in 2004 in Richterswil by Marius and Manuel; it has grown from a small hosting startup into a Swiss hosting provider with its own datacenters and the Website Creator product.
Website Creator.summary_abuse: As a paid website-builder and hosting platform with custom domains, abuse is mostly limited to scam landing pages, phishing sites, and fake storefronts rather than mass anonymous use. The lack of a free tier lowers abuse pressure, and I found no sign of a major abuse-specific reputation problem in the pages reviewed.

Website Speedy.history: Founded in 2023 by MakkPress as a SaaS website-speed optimization tool; no notable acquisitions or deprecation events since.
Website Speedy.summary_abuse: Website Speedy has no meaningful abuse signal; it is a site-performance SaaS rather than a hosting, messaging, or URL-shortening platform. Any misuse would be incidental, such as scam sites also wanting faster page loads, but the product does not appear to offer anonymous publishing, custom-domain hosting, or bulk-messaging features that typically drive abuse.

WebSite X5.history: WebSite X5 is a desktop website creation software developed by Italian company Incomedia since 1998, targeting non-technical users who want to create and publish websites locally before uploading them to hosting.
WebSite X5.summary_abuse: WebSite X5 is a desktop website builder software with no notable abuse signal in public sources.

WebsiteBaker.history: WebsiteBaker was first released in December 2004 by Ryan Djurovich as a PHP-based open-source CMS. The project experienced community tensions over development direction, which led to the Lepton-CMS fork. After the unexpected death of the lead developer, a community team of 10 took over. The project continues to receive maintenance releases as of late 2024.
WebsiteBaker.summary_abuse: WebsiteBaker is an open-source self-hosted CMS with no notable abuse signal. As a self-hosted PHP CMS with no hosted service surface, it has no infrastructure that fraudsters would exploit directly.

WebsiteBuilder.history: WebsiteBuilder.com is a drag-and-drop website builder brand that became part of the Endurance International Group (later Newfold Digital) portfolio, alongside brands like HostGator and Bluehost. Newfold Digital was acquired by Clearlake Capital in 2021. The platform has seen no notable ownership changes beyond the broader Newfold corporate structure.
WebsiteBuilder.summary_abuse: WebsiteBuilder.com is a drag-and-drop website builder offering custom domain support on paid plans. Like most consumer website builders, it has been observed in phishing and fake-storefront campaigns where scammers create credential-harvesting pages. The parent company Newfold Digital maintains standard acceptable use policies and responds to abuse reports, though the platform's large user base and ease of setup create some surface for abuse. No major public abuse incidents specific to WebsiteBuilder.com have been documented compared to the broader website-builder industry pattern.

WebSocket.history: WebSocket is a communication protocol standardized by the IETF in 2011 (RFC 6455), providing full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection. Widely adopted for real-time web applications.
WebSocket.summary_abuse: WebSocket is a foundational network protocol with no notable abuse signal inherent to the standard itself.

Websocket.history: WebSocket is a communication protocol standardized as RFC 6455 by the IETF in December 2011. It provides full-duplex communication channels over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time bidirectional data transfer between web clients and servers. The protocol was developed to address limitations of HTTP for real-time applications. It is universally supported in all modern web browsers and has been widely adopted for applications including live chat, gaming, financial tickers, and collaborative editing. No protocol-level deprecation events exist.
Websocket.summary_abuse: WebSocket is a foundational communication protocol with no meaningful abuse signal in its own right. As a transport mechanism, it can be used by applications for both legitimate and malicious purposes, but the protocol itself has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters directly target. Abuse patterns are specific to the applications using WebSocket, not the protocol.

WebSphere.history: IBM WebSphere Application Server was first released in 1998 as IBM's flagship Java EE application server for enterprise deployments. It became widely adopted in large enterprise and government IT environments. Over time IBM has also offered Liberty editions and Open Liberty as lightweight variants, while continuing to maintain the traditional WebSphere for legacy enterprise workloads.
WebSphere.summary_abuse: IBM WebSphere is an enterprise application server with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal. It is deployed in controlled enterprise data center environments.

WebsPlanet.history: WebsPlanet was founded in 2004 in Tel Aviv, Israel, and provides white-label website builder platforms for telecoms, web hosting providers, and SMBs. The company targets service providers wanting to bundle DIY and DIFM website creation as a commercial offering.
WebsPlanet.summary_abuse: WebsPlanet is a B2B white-label website builder platform sold to telecoms and hosting companies with no notable consumer-facing abuse signal.

WebSTAT.history: WebSTAT is a web analytics platform that has been operating since the early 2000s, offering simplified website visitor tracking and statistics reporting.
WebSTAT.summary_abuse: WebSTAT is a web analytics service with no notable abuse signal.

Webstudio.history: Webstudio's public GitHub repository was created in March 2022, and the project has stayed under active development as an open-source visual website builder with a hosted cloud offering.
Webstudio.summary_abuse: Webstudio is a page builder, so it can be used to publish scam or phishing landing pages, but the hosted plan limits the free tier to a wstd.io subdomain and paid custom domains, which reduces low-friction abuse. The company also publishes a fair-usage policy and a DPA; abuse risk is mainly from self-hosted exports or paid sites rather than a consumer-facing abuse surface, and there is no well-known public abuse program or major incident tied to the product.

WebToffee Stripe Payment Plugin for WooC.history: WebToffee is an Indian WooCommerce plugin company founded around 2015, offering a range of WooCommerce add-ons including their Stripe payment gateway plugin for processing payments on WooCommerce stores.
WebToffee Stripe Payment Plugin for WooC.summary_abuse: WebToffee's Stripe plugin is a payment processing extension for WooCommerce. Plugin-based payment integrations can be targets for credential theft if compromised, but no specific incidents are documented for this plugin.

Webtrends.history: Webtrends was founded in 1993 by W. Glen Boyd and Eli Shapira as E.G. Software, pioneering web analytics with a Log Analyzer product. NetIQ acquired the company in 2001, then sold it to private equity firm Francisco Partners in 2005. In 2016, Webtrends sold its Infinity Analytics product to Oracle, and in 2018 a management buyout spun off the testing and personalization division as the separate company Webtrends Optimize.
Webtrends.summary_abuse: Webtrends is a web analytics and marketing platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B enterprise analytics tool used by large organizations to measure digital marketing performance, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target. Webtrends collects extensive browsing and behavioral data as part of its core analytics function, which is a privacy consideration but not an abuse vector.

Webware.history: Webware was founded in 2015 by Cory York in Toronto, Canada as an all-in-one digital marketing platform for small to medium-sized businesses. The company grew to over 200 employees serving 3500+ active clients across North America. Cory York serves on the Forbes Council. No acquisitions or ownership changes have occurred.
Webware.summary_abuse: Webware is an all-in-one marketing platform for SMBs offering website building, SEO, social media management, email marketing, CRM, and paid ads management. It has low abuse signal as a controlled SaaS platform serving legitimate local businesses through a sales-based onboarding process. The platform does not expose anonymous signup or user-generated content hosting that fraudsters typically exploit. Based in Toronto with 200+ employees. No notable abuse incidents or security breaches have been publicly reported.

WebWave.history: WebWave is a Polish drag-and-drop website builder and CMS founded around 2016, offering a free tier and paid plans for building websites without coding.
WebWave.summary_abuse: WebWave is a hosted website builder with a free tier. Like other free website builders, it has theoretical risk of fake-page creation, but no notable abuse incidents are documented.

Webx.history: Webx says it has been operating in Pakistan’s ecommerce market for 12 years, implying a 2013 launch. It remains independently run from Karachi, with no notable acquisition or rebrand publicly noted.
Webx.summary_abuse: Webx has some abuse risk because it is a hosted storefront platform with custom domains, a free trial, and built-in ecommerce features that can be used for fake stores or scam sales pages. Legitimate use appears to dominate, and the company has visible support, terms, and a contact path for complaints, so this is a moderate rather than severe abuse signal.

WEBXPAY.history: WEBXPAY is a Sri Lanka-based online payment gateway that facilitates ecommerce transactions in South Asia. It is a regional payment processor with a focus on the Sri Lankan market.
WEBXPAY.summary_abuse: WEBXPAY is a regional payment gateway operating in South Asia. Payment gateways carry inherent fraud exposure such as card-testing and fraudulent merchant onboarding, but no specific large-scale abuse incidents are documented for WEBXPAY.

Webydo.history: Founded 2010 as Webydo Inc.; provides a no-code visual website builder and hosting platform for designers and agencies. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Webydo.summary_abuse: Webydo is a SaaS website builder that hosts user sites on its platform. As a page builder with free-tier custom-domain hosting, it carries inherent abuse risk — fraudulent storefronts and phishing pages can be created via free trial or paid plans. The vendor publishes a privacy policy and terms of service but no dedicated abuse-reporting mechanism or security.txt was found. No major public incidents involving Webydo were identified in security research. The vast majority of use is legitimate design and agency work.

Webyn.history: Founded in March 2023 by Vincent Oliveira and Alexandre Farhat in Bois-Colombes, France. Raised €1.2M seed funding in January 2024, began commercialization in February 2024, and reached 100 clients by January 2025. No notable ownership changes, forks, or deprecation events since founding.
Webyn.summary_abuse: Webyn is a CRO (conversion rate optimization) SaaS platform that injects JavaScript into client websites to analyze visitor behavior and run A/B tests. It has no notable abuse signal — it is a legitimate marketing analytics and personalization tool used by e-commerce sites. The service is fully hosted with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target. No documented incidents of abuse were found in public sources. The company publishes a GDPR-compliant privacy policy and is subject to French data protection law.

WebZi.history: WebZi (formerly Artehosting) was founded in 2006 by Daniel Rivera in Mexico. The company initially resold hosting services from other Mexican providers before establishing itself as a legal corporation (Artehosting S.A. de C.V.) in 2011 and building its own infrastructure with leased servers. By 2015 the company had become a market leader in Mexico. In 2021 Artehosting opened its own data center in Mexico. In 2023 the company rebranded to WebZi, retaining its existing product capabilities under a new brand identity.
WebZi.summary_abuse: WebZi is a Mexican web hosting and website builder platform with a modest abuse risk profile. Website builder platforms that offer free or low-cost hosting under shared infrastructure can occasionally be used for phishing pages or spam landing pages, though this is not specific to WebZi. No notable public incidents or documented abuse campaigns are specifically attributed to WebZi or its predecessor Artehosting.

Webzie.history: Public pages show a 2023 copyright and an ongoing global/Israel site split, but no notable acquisitions, rebrands, or deprecation events are visible.
Webzie.summary_abuse: Webzie is a normal website/ecommerce builder, but like similar builders it can host arbitrary landing pages on custom domains, which makes it usable for scam or phishing sites. It offers public signup and a free trial, lowering the barrier to abuse; I found no public sign of a major abuse problem or a clearly published anti-abuse program, so the risk looks moderate rather than extreme.

Weebly.history: Founded in 2006 in San Francisco by David Rusenko, Chris Fanini, and Dan Veltri as a drag-and-drop website builder. Grew to become one of the leading website building platforms alongside Wix and Squarespace. In 2018, Square, Inc. (now Block, Inc.) acquired Weebly for $365 million. The platform continues to operate under Block, offering integrated ecommerce and website building tools with a freemium pricing model.
Weebly.summary_abuse: Weebly's free tier with custom domain support makes it one of the most commonly abused website builders for phishing page hosting. Scammers exploit the free subdomain (weebly.com) and free custom domain options to host fake login pages, brand impersonation sites, and credential harvesting forms. The platform has been frequently cited in phishing reports and security research. Block (Square) maintains abuse reporting channels and generally responds to takedown requests, but the ease of free account creation and site publishing means new phishing sites appear regularly. This combination of high abuse volume and visible (if imperfect) vendor response places Weebly in a moderate-to-high abuse category.

WeeBnB.history: Founded 2015 as WEEDIGITAL SAS (trading as WeeBnB) by Xavier Mallein in Gradignan, France. Commercial launch March 2016. No notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events since.
WeeBnB.summary_abuse: WeeBnB has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche SaaS for French vacation rental owners, distributed exclusively through accredited local tourism offices (Offices de Tourisme). Anonymous signup is not possible — access requires tourism-office affiliation. The platform provides website creation, calendar synchronization, and booking tools for legitimate short-term rental hosts. Abuse risk is negligible due to the restricted distribution model. The vendor maintains standard terms of use prohibiting illegal content and can be contacted for takedown requests. No public incidents of abuse have been documented.

Weekdone.history: Founded 2009 in Tartu, Estonia by Juri Kaljundi, Janek Hiis, and Veli Veromann. Backed by KIMA Ventures and angel investors including Skype founding engineer Ahti Heinla. The company builds OKR and weekly progress reporting software for SMBs; no notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events since founding.
Weekdone.summary_abuse: Weekdone has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B OKR and team management SaaS with no consumer-facing surface for hosting content, URL redirection, mass-email, or file distribution that fraudsters typically exploit. The service requires a valid email and company account behind a login, with no anonymous signup or custom-domain hosting features. No documented phishing, scam, or malware abuse tied to the platform has been publicly reported.

Weezbe.history: Launched in 2009 by Nukium (French SAS based in Lavérune), a web agency founded by Samuel Sanchez. The platform has remained under Nukium's operation without notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events since launch.
Weezbe.summary_abuse: Weezbe is a small French hosted ecommerce platform with low abuse signal. As a storefront builder offering a free trial and custom domain support, it could be used for fake storefronts, but no significant pattern of such abuse is publicly documented. The platform serves hundreds of merchants primarily in French-speaking Europe, requires human onboarding, and its small scale and regional focus limit its attractiveness for broad fraud campaigns. The vendor offers direct support but publishes no dedicated anti-abuse policy or automated abuse-detection system.

Weezevent.history: Founded in April 2008 in France by Pierre-Henri Deballon and Sebastien Tonglet as a self-service online ticketing SaaS. Raised 1M EUR in 2011. Acquired Wooticket in 2012 and merged with cashless provider Nemopay in 2015, with Vente-Privee investing the same year. Regained independence and merged with Playpass in 2021. Acquired Recrewteer in 2023. Participated in Paris 2024 Olympics operations and joined French Tech Next 40 in 2024.
Weezevent.summary_abuse: Weezevent has low abuse signal typical of event ticketing platforms. Potential abuse vectors include fake-event listings and ticket-resale scams, but the platform mitigates through organizer verification, GIE Cartes Bancaires payment certification (since 2009), sequestered bank accounts for ticket funds, and CNIL-registered data processing. No major public abuse incidents are documented. Free tier exists for free events, limiting financial abuse surface. The vendor publishes a security page detailing regular audits, SSL/3DSecure encryption, and AWS hosting. Takedown responsiveness is not independently documented but the regulated payment processing environment implies standard compliance obligations.

Weglot.history: Weglot was founded in Paris, France in 2016 by Augustin Prot and Remy Berda as a no-code SaaS website translation and localization platform. The company has grown to serve tens of thousands of websites and raised venture capital to scale its multilingual content management offering.
Weglot.summary_abuse: Weglot has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B translation SaaS that injects translated content into websites; it does not host user content or allow custom domain creation that would appeal to phishing operators. Its primary role is legitimate website localization.

Wehaa.history: Founded circa 2011 as a digital revenue solutions provider for media companies in North America. Has grown over 15 years into a multi-national provider serving hundreds of media companies including the Washington Post, Los Angeles Times, and Seattle Times. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions documented.
Wehaa.summary_abuse: Wehaa is a B2B ad-tech platform serving verified media companies, which limits its abuse surface. Services are sold as white-label solutions to established media organizations, not directly to consumers. As an advertising platform, some risk of serving ads alongside questionable content exists but is mediated by the B2B media-company customer base. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Welcart.history: Welcart was launched around 2012 as a free open-source WordPress ecommerce plugin developed by Welcart Inc. It grew to become the number-one ecommerce plugin in Japan by market share, powering over 26,000 active sites. The plugin follows a freemium model with a free core and paid extension plugins and themes. The parent company is Collne Inc. No acquisitions or major rebranding events are documented.
Welcart.summary_abuse: Welcart is an open-source WordPress ecommerce plugin with low abuse risk. As a self-hosted plugin running on the merchant's own server, it does not expose consumer-facing services that fraudsters directly abuse. Any ecommerce platform can be used for fraudulent storefronts, but Welcart's primary market is legitimate Japanese merchants. The plugin has no free hosted tier, no anonymous signup, and no mass-email features. Welcart Inc. publishes a privacy policy, privacy mark certification, and terms of service. The site also warns users about phishing emails impersonating Welcart. No major abuse incidents specifically tied to Welcart have been publicly documented.

WeltPixel Pearl Theme.history: WeltPixel was founded in 2016 by Virgil Ghic in Cluj-Napoca, Romania. Pearl Theme has remained actively maintained and was updated in January 2026.
WeltPixel Pearl Theme.summary_abuse: Pearl Theme has no notable abuse signal; it is a self-hosted Magento theme for legitimate storefronts, not hosting, shortening, or other consumer-facing infrastructure that scammers usually target.

WEN Themes Education Hub.history: WEN Themes Education Hub is a WordPress theme for educational institutions developed by WEN Themes, a small theme shop. No notable ownership changes or significant events on record.
WEN Themes Education Hub.summary_abuse: WEN Themes Education Hub is a WordPress theme for school websites with no abuse signal. It is a self-hosted template and not a hosted service exploitable for fraud.

WEN Themes Signify Dark.history: Signify Dark is a free dark WordPress theme for blogs and corporate sites developed by WEN Themes. It is a free theme distributed on WordPress.org with no notable history.
WEN Themes Signify Dark.summary_abuse: WEN Themes Signify Dark is a free WordPress theme with no abuse signal. It is a self-hosted template and not a hosted service.

Wepay.history: WePay was founded in 2008 as an integrated payments platform targeting software platforms and crowdfunding services. In December 2017, JPMorgan Chase completed the acquisition of WePay for a reported value of up to $400 million, making it a Chase subsidiary focused on embedded payments for small businesses and software platforms.
Wepay.summary_abuse: WePay is a payment processing platform backed by JPMorgan Chase with strong fraud controls and KYC requirements for merchant onboarding. It is occasionally implicated in fraud disputes as payment processors are, but legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates and Chase's compliance infrastructure ensures strong anti-abuse posture. No documented pattern of abuse-friendly policies.

Weply.history: Founded in 2018 in Copenhagen. It expanded to Sweden in 2020, the Netherlands in 2022, and Norway after securing funding; no major rebrands or acquisitions are noted.
Weply.summary_abuse: Weply has limited abuse signal: as a hosted live-chat widget, it can be used on scam or impersonation landing pages to look legitimate or capture leads, but its main use is ordinary B2B lead generation. Its normal SaaS posture and published privacy policy suggest standard monitoring and support rather than abuse-friendly infrastructure.

WeTravel.history: WeTravel was founded around 2016 as a booking and payment solution specifically for group travel organizers, tour operators, and travel companies.
WeTravel.summary_abuse: WeTravel is a travel booking and payment platform for tour operators. No notable abuse signal in public sources. Payment platforms in travel can be targets for chargeback fraud but no specific incidents are documented for this provider.

WeWeb.history: WeWeb was founded in 2019 in France as a no-code front-end builder that connects to existing backends and APIs, positioning itself as developer-friendly for building web applications without writing code.
WeWeb.summary_abuse: WeWeb is a no-code web application builder. No notable abuse signal in public sources; it is used by developers and agencies to build legitimate web applications.

Wfolio.history: Wfolio is a website builder and cloud drive platform for photographers. The company origins appear to be Eastern European, with a customer base spread across Latvia, Ukraine, Germany, and other European countries. The platform offers portfolio websites, photo galleries, and client sales tools for photographers. The company's exact founding year and headquarters are not publicly documented.
Wfolio.summary_abuse: Wfolio is a photography portfolio website builder with a low abuse signal. The platform creates personal portfolio sites for photographers and does not offer generic free hosting under custom domains. No documented phishing, scam hosting, or fraud incidents are associated with Wfolio.

Whatfix.history: Founded in 2014 by Khadim Batti and Vara Kumar after evolving from an earlier product called SearchEnabler. It later expanded from a digital adoption platform into a multi-product suite and raised a Series E in 2024.
Whatfix.summary_abuse: Whatfix has no notable abuse signal; it is an enterprise digital adoption platform used inside customer-owned business applications rather than a public hosting, redirect, or content-publishing surface. The trust center publishes an acceptable-usage policy and vulnerability disclosure program, which points to a normal enterprise SaaS abuse posture with active enforcement rather than scam-friendly openness.

WhatsApp Business Chat.history: Launched in 2018 as WhatsApp’s business-focused app and now operated under Meta as part of the WhatsApp business messaging stack.
WhatsApp Business Chat.summary_abuse: WhatsApp Business is heavily used for legitimate customer support and sales, but it is also abused for scam outreach, spam, and phishing because phone-number-based accounts can look trusted and messages reach users directly. Meta says it proactively detects and removes most spam and scam accounts, and its security.txt points to public bug-bounty and disclosure channels.

Wheel of Popups.history: Built by Earn Coupon d.o.o. as a spin-to-win Shopify popup tool, then expanded into a broader popup and lead-capture suite. The company says its founders had already started five startups, including one earlier acquisition.
Wheel of Popups.summary_abuse: Wheel of Popups is mainly a legitimate marketing popup builder, and I didn’t find a notable public abuse pattern or abuse-only usage. Like similar lead-capture tools, it could be repurposed for deceptive overlays or scam promotions on customer sites, but there’s no sign that this is a major share of use.

Wheelio.history: Launched in 2017 by IT Trading d.o.o.; the product has stayed under the same vendor with no notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Wheelio.summary_abuse: Wheelio has limited abuse signal overall: it is a mainstream ecommerce pop-up and lead-capture app, so scammers could misuse its custom gamified forms to dress up scam storefronts or collect emails, but the public record is dominated by normal merchant use rather than abuse. The vendor publishes a privacy policy and has a visible support presence, which lowers the risk of persistent abuse compared with anonymous page-builders or free hosting tools.

Wheely Sales.history: Wheely Sales is a gamified exit-intent email opt-in popup tool using spin-to-win mechanics; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Wheely Sales.summary_abuse: Wheely Sales is a gamified email capture popup tool. Spin-to-win popups can be used as dark patterns to manipulate users into providing email addresses, but no specific fraud incidents are documented.

Whelp.history: Whelp is an omnichannel customer support SaaS platform founded by Seymur Rasulov (Whelp, Inc.). The platform provides AI-powered chatbots, shared inbox, CRM, and outbound communication tools across channels including WhatsApp, SMS, email, and social media.
Whelp.summary_abuse: Whelp is a B2B customer support platform providing omnichannel inbox, chatbot, and outbound messaging tools. As a communications platform, it enables bulk messaging across WhatsApp, Telegram, email, and Facebook Messenger. The platform requires business registration and provides standard security features. Abuse potential is moderate and consistent with other customer communication platforms.

Wherewolf.history: Founded around 2016 in Queenstown, New Zealand by Ben Calder and Wulf Solter. Calder, running a pub crawl business, sought a mobile guest check-in solution and kept asking 'where is Wulf?' for technical help, giving the company its name. Wherewolf expanded from a local Queenstown solution to supporting thousands of tour, sports, and attraction businesses globally, processing over 200M waivers. The company opened a US office in Denver, Colorado but remains privately held with co-founders in leadership.
Wherewolf.summary_abuse: Wherewolf is a digital waiver and guest registration platform serving legitimate tour operators, attractions, and sports businesses. It has minimal abuse signal: its customer base of real, verified businesses with booking integrations makes it an unattractive vector for phishing or fraud. The platform collects PII (names, emails, IDs) and offers email/SMS marketing features, but requires business registration and typically paid plans for meaningful usage. Terms of Use clause 11 prohibits illegal or malicious use. The company has GDPR compliance measures including a published Data Processing Agreement. No notable public incidents of platform abuse by scammers have been documented.

Whistl.history: Whistl traces its origins to Circular Distributors Ltd (founded 1958), a direct marketing business acquired by TNT Group in 2001. It rebranded as TNT Post in 2006 and later as Whistl in September 2014 following the spin-off of PostNL from TNT Express. After a failed expansion into final-mile delivery (suspended May 2015, with 1,800 roles affected), management completed a buyout from PostNL in October 2015. PostNL retained a 17.5% stake. The company then diversified into parcel delivery, fulfilment, and contact centre services through acquisitions including Prism DM (2017), Parcelhub (2018), Spark Ecommerce (2018), Clientbase Fulfilment (2020), and Relish (2021).
Whistl.summary_abuse: Whistl is a UK B2B logistics provider handling bulk mail, parcel delivery, and fulfilment — not a public-facing platform. Its abuse surface is limited to ordinary carrier-related fraud (lost-item scams, delivery-fee phishing) common to all postal operators. As a contracted service for established businesses with no free tier, anonymous signup, or custom-domain hosting, it offers no features that fraudsters preferentially exploit. Whistl publishes an acceptable-use policy and is regulated by UK postal authorities. No documented pattern of systematic abuse specific to this provider was identified.

Whitelabel MD.history: Founded 2022 as Whitelabel MD LLC. Provides a white-label turnkey platform for creating e-commerce telemedicine websites with on-demand prescription fulfillment. Integrates telemedicine providers with nationwide mail-order pharmacies. Team has over 30 years combined experience in healthcare services and technology. LegitScript verified for healthcare compliance.
Whitelabel MD.summary_abuse: Whitelabel MD is a white-label telemedicine e-commerce platform enabling creation of online prescription fulfillment websites. The platform handles prescription medications, which carries inherent regulatory and abuse risks including potential for unlicensed prescribing or controlled substance diversion. The company uses LegitScript verification and has privacy and terms policies in place. As a turnkey provider powering other businesses' telemedicine sites, abuse depends on how downstream operators use the platform. No notable public abuse incidents have been documented.

Whitespark SEO.history: Founded in 2005 by Darren Shaw in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, initially offering web design and SEO services. Over two decades it grew into a provider of local SEO software and managed services, serving over 100,000 enterprises, agencies, and small businesses globally. No acquisitions, rebrands, or deprecation events have occurred.
Whitespark SEO.summary_abuse: Whitespark's SEO services have minimal abuse signal.
